segments.text: At least 33 people are dead. That is the body count Scott Carney published in July 2025 — linked to one breathing method, one shared mechanism, one failure of the discourse to say plainly what the physiology had already documented.

And Carney is not a critic who came in hostile. He spent a decade as Wim Hof's most prominent scientific advocate. He wrote the 2017 book What Doesn't Kill Us. He became a certified WHM instructor. He trained thousands of practitioners.

Ten years as a true believer — and then in 2023 he started publishing a different story. By July 2025 he had written: 'Since I first met Hof in 2013 he has become increasingly erratic and even dangerous.' That sentence. From that man.

That's the turn that frames this transmission. Not an outside critic landing punches. Someone who spent a decade trying to prove the opposite, and couldn't. The question we're here to answer is: what exactly did those 33 deaths share?

And before we go further — the peer-reviewed science on the Wim Hof Method is not in dispute here. The 2014 Radboud paper is real. Twelve men, four days of training, measurable modulation of the innate immune response. We covered that in a previous transmission.

The science stands. What we're documenting today is a specific application failure — one that has a published physiological mechanism, is completely avoidable, and was not communicated clearly enough to the practitioners who died following it.
So — the mechanism. Walk me through it.

The WHM breathing protocol is 30 to 40 cycles of deep, rhythmic hyperventilation followed by a breath-hold. That hyperventilation strips carbon dioxide from the blood faster than the body replaces it. Resting blood CO2 — measured as PaCO2 — sits around 40 millimeters of mercury. After a full WHM round, it drops to as low as 17 to 20.
And CO2 is the signal.

Exactly. The brain's trigger to breathe is not falling oxygen — it is rising CO2. Bart and Lau, 2023, in StatPearls via NCBI, put it directly: 'Hyperventilation before breath-holding lowers the arterial partial pressure of CO2 and delays the re-emergence of the stimulus to breathe.'
So you don't feel yourself running out of oxygen.

You feel nothing. Kumar and Ng, 2010, Medical Journal of Australia: 'Loss of consciousness may ensue without forewarning because the respiratory stimulus from hypoxaemia is weak.' On dry land, you faint. Underwater, you drown. The body gives no alarm.
This is shallow-water blackout. It exists in the freediving literature independently of Wim Hof.

It's well described. And it does not discriminate. Trained athletes, former soldiers, healthy adults, teenagers — the documented cases include all of them. Fitness level and prior experience with the method did not protect anyone.
And then there's the cold component layered on top of that.

Mike Tipton, Professor of Human and Applied Physiology at the University of Portsmouth, reviewed this in The Lancet in 2003. Cold shock produces an immediate gasp reflex and uncontrolled hyperventilation. His words: 'A fall in skin temperature elicits a powerful cardiorespiratory response, termed cold shock, comprising an initial gasp, hypertension, and hyperventilation despite a profound hypocapnia.'
Despite a profound hypocapnia — meaning this happens even when CO2 is already depleted. The system forces a gasp anyway.

Datta and Tipton, 2006, Journal of Applied Physiology, documented the neural pathways: 'The respiratory responses to skin cooling override both conscious and other autonomic respiratory controls and may act as a precursor to drowning.' You cannot suppress this reflex. It happens before you can choose.
So WHM breathing followed by cold-water submersion is two independent drowning mechanisms, stacked.

Tipton described the combination as — and these are his exact words — 'about the most dangerous thing you can do when you go into cold water.' Two triggers. Either one alone is dangerous. Together, the margin collapses.
Now the cases. Carney's investigation started with 13 drownings in his June 2023 piece. By January 2024 it was 21 deaths and 18 injuries. By July 2025 — at least 33 deaths. Let's name some of them.

Andrew Encinas, 27, Labor Day 2019. Social media entrepreneur, backyard pool party. He told family he wanted to do his Wim Hof method in the pool. Children at the party noticed he appeared to be sleeping in the shallow end. His brother found him in a meditative position underwater — hands clasped in front of his chest, unresponsive. No struggle. No call for help.
That's the blackout pattern. No warning, no attempt to surface.

Christopher Kuyvenhoven, 23, January 2019. Former soldier, aspiring Navy SEAL candidate. Found unresponsive in a hot tub at Western Washington University after practicing WHM breathing before a breath-hold. He had been submerged for approximately 20 minutes before discovery. He did not survive.
A Navy SEAL candidate. Physically elite. It didn't matter.

Nova Xavier, July 17, 2023. When his mother raised concerns about the hyperventilation exercises, Nova told her: 'Of course it's safe, mom. It's Wim Hof.' He had purchased Hof's Classic Course online for $100. At a community pool, his mother, Catherine Xavier, was timing his breath holds when he failed to resurface. Carney documented it: 'A few minutes later she noticed that he hadn't come up for air. Her son was already dead.'
His mother was there. She was timing him. And he was still gone before anyone knew.

Madelyn Rose Metzger, 17, August 2022, Long Beach, California. Found face-down in the family pool. Her father, Raphael Metzger, filed a $67 million civil suit against Wim Hof and Innerfire. The case was dismissed July 2024 — not on a finding that the method is safe, but on a failure to prove causation in that specific case to the court's evidentiary threshold.
And Hof's own attorney made that distinction explicitly on the record.

Directly. The attorney's words, as reported by DutchNews.nl in 2024: 'The question is not whether or not Hof's breathing exercises can lead to drowning.' That is an extraordinary sentence to come from the defense.
The Dutch deaths came earlier. Four practitioners reported by Het Parool in 2016.

And the Innerfire response at that time — from Enahm Hof, Wim's son and Innerfire CEO — was: 'Pretty silly — but then you shouldn't do the exercises under water.' That's it. Relatives had just lost family members in swimming pools after breathing sessions, and the response was: pretty silly.
And the rule wasn't in the course materials.

That is the gap this transmission is documenting. Innerfire's current position, stated by spokesperson Isabelle Hof in 2024, is: 'Since we heard about people potentially drowning because of malpractice of the WHM breathing in water in 2015, we warn people.' Current materials carry the warning. The word 'malpractice' is doing a lot of work there.
Because the people who died weren't going off-script. They were following the official course.

Nova Xavier paid $100 for the official Hof online course. Carney documented that Hof himself, at live public events, conflated the breathwork and water-submersion elements without clear safety separation — this was captured on video. The Innerfire line that these were unauthorized third-party instructors does not hold against the documented cases.
And the Facebook post.

A widow posted about her husband's death in 2016. Innerfire deleted it from their page. Enahm Hof's explanation was that the post was 'an attack without any evidence.' The husband was dead. The post was gone.
We need to stop here and say something clearly — because people may be listening to this while they are near water, or planning a session today. The hard rules. Say them straight.
Here they are, with no hedge. One: never practice WHM breathing in water. Not a pool. Not a bathtub. Not a lake, river, or ocean. Full stop.
That includes showers. That includes any configuration where unconsciousness means your airway goes under.

Two: never practice WHM breathing near water in a configuration where loss of consciousness would result in submersion. Three: never practice WHM breathing before any underwater activity — not before swimming laps, not before diving, not before any breath-hold exercise in water.
Even if you intend to wait a few minutes. The CO2 depletion persists.

Four: practice the breathing rounds lying down on a solid surface. Bed, floor, yoga mat. Fainting during the breathing rounds is documented. If you faint on the floor, you are safe. Five: do not drive immediately after a session — altered CO2 levels, potential lightheadedness.
And the contraindication list.

Cardiovascular disease. Uncontrolled hypertension. Epilepsy or history of seizures. Pregnancy. History of unexplained syncope — fainting without a clear cause. Any condition affecting respiratory control. These are hard stops. Consult a physician before beginning. And cold exposure — ice baths, cold showers — is a separate element with its own cardiac risk profile. Do not run the breathing rounds immediately before entering cold water.
The rule is not complicated. It is just absent from too many places where practitioners learned this method.
Nova Xavier's mother purchased an official course. He followed the instructions. He died. That is the sentence that grounds all of this.
So what can we actually say, and what can't we?

We can say: voluntary hyperventilation followed by breath-holding underwater produces a well-described physiological state — hypocapnia followed by hypoxic blackout — that causes loss of consciousness without warning. This is documented in the diving-medicine literature independently of any Wim Hof research.
It predates Wim Hof. It will outlast him. The physiology does not care about the brand.

We can say: at least 33 deaths have been linked by investigative reporting to WHM practice near or in water. The pattern is consistent across cases — no struggle, no cry for help, found in a meditative or relaxed position. We can say: Innerfire added safety warnings after 2015 deaths. Those warnings were not present when practitioners following 2015 and 2016 official materials drowned.
And the California case.

We can say: the California civil case against Innerfire was dismissed on evidentiary grounds, not on a finding that the method is safe. The peer-reviewed immunological science — the 2014 PNAS paper, the 2018 Wayne State fMRI study — remains valid and unrebutted. The breathwork on a dry mat, in a healthy adult without contraindications, does not carry the same risk profile.
What we can't say.

We can't say that Wim Hof personally caused any specific death. Courts have not found this. The investigation has not proven it. We can't say that Innerfire knowingly suppressed safety information — we can say that a warning post from a widow was deleted and that the deaths preceded adequate warnings in course materials. We can't say the dry-land breathing protocol is without risk — fainting is documented, seizure-like activity has been reported in group settings.

The discourse sorted itself into two useless camps. Advocates who treat any criticism as an attack on the science. Critics who dismiss the science because the application has caused deaths. Both camps got people killed.

The science is documented. The deaths are documented. The mechanism connecting them is documented. If you practiced this method without knowing these rules — if you did the breathing rounds in a pool, in a bathtub, before a swim session — you were failed by the discourse.
The discourse is fixed now. The question is what you do with it.

Listeners looking at breathwork with a different risk profile — our previous transmission on the Wim Hof Method covers what the peer-reviewed science actually supports, including the Radboud immunology and what the 2018 fMRI data showed. That's the place to start.
